notes: Official Monarch product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Here are the main areas most households look at when trying to lower housing costs without taking on a lot of risk. I’ll keep it practical.

If you want, I can also look at your actual mortgage and housing expenses to make this specific to you.

1. Get really clear on your current mortgage

First, gather:

Current balance
Interest rate
Years left on the loan
Monthly principal & interest payment
Type: fixed vs adjustable, and if adjustable, when it resets and how it’s capped

Once you have that, you can look at:

A. Rate-and-term refinance (only if it truly lowers cost)

Lower risk if:

You get a meaningfully lower rate and/or
You shorten the term without raising the payment too much, or
You extend the term a bit to lower the payment, but keep total interest reasonable

Be careful about:

Resetting to a new 30-year term late in your current loan (total interest can jump a lot)
High closing costs that eat up the savings
Cash-out refis that increase your balance unless you have a very clear, low-risk reason

Quick gut check:

Compare new payment vs current payment
Break-even time = closing costs ÷ monthly savings

If you might move or refinance again before that break-even point, it may not be worth it.

2. Lower your payment without refinancing
A. Remove PMI (if you have it)

If you put less than 20% down, you might be paying mortgage insurance.

You could:

Check your current loan-to-value (LTV) using a realistic home value (not just a rosy estimate)
Ask your servicer what’s required to remove PMI (often an appraisal once you’re at 78–80% LTV)

This can sometimes save hundreds per month with no extra risk.

B. Recast your mortgage

If you have or expect a lump sum (bonus, inheritance, sale of something), some lenders allow a recast:

You make a large principal payment
They re-amortize the loan at the same rate and term
Your monthly payment drops

This is usually lower risk than a full refinance (often low fees, no new credit check), but you are tying up more cash in home equity.

3. Cut non-mortgage housing costs

These are often lower risk and easier to undo.

A. Home insurance
Shop around for the same coverage levels and deductibles
Consider a slightly higher deductible if you have an emergency fund
Remove riders you don’t truly need

Avoid underinsuring the structure or liability coverage just to shave a few dollars.

B. Property taxes
Check if your assessed value looks high vs similar homes
Look into:
Homestead exemptions
Senior / veteran / low-income exemptions (if they apply)
If the assessment seems off, you could consider an appeal using recent comparable sales.
C. Utilities and services

Look at:

Heating/cooling efficiency (smart thermostat, sealing leaks, LED bulbs)
Internet/cable: downgrade speed, cut cable, or renegotiate
Lawn care, pest control, cleaning services: decide what really matters to you vs what you could DIY or do less often

Each one might be small, but together they can meaningfully lower your overall “housing” spend.

4. Use your home to offset costs (carefully)

These add complexity but can help if done thoughtfully:

Rent out a room or part of the home (long-term tenant or trusted housemate)
Occasional short-term rental (if local rules and your comfort level allow)
Rent out a parking space or storage area if you’re in a dense area

Key things:

Know local laws and HOA rules
Be realistic about vacancy, wear and tear, and how you feel about sharing space
5. Cash-flow planning and safety guardrails

To avoid turning a housing fix into a bigger problem:

Keep an emergency fund (ideally 3–6 months of expenses)
Avoid pulling from retirement savings or taking on high-interest debt to “fix” housing costs
Be cautious about using HELOCs for non-essential spending

Big picture:
Start with understanding your current mortgage, then look at PMI/recast options, insurance/tax/utility tweaks, and only then consider bigger moves like refis or renting out space.

If you’d like more tailored guidance, I can review:

Your current mortgage rate, balance, and payment
Your property tax, insurance, utilities, and home-related subscriptions

Then I can outline the 2–3 most realistic ways for you specifically to lower your housing costs.
